title: Minister on board to slow AI juggernaut
full_text:
The federal government has welcomed Anthropic’s push to slow artificial intelligence development, despite no clear strategy for preventing humans ultimately losing control of the technology.
Anthropic, which has a valuation of almost $US1 trillion ($1.42 trillion) and is preparing a blockbuster float, says AI is close to “building itself”, or “recursive self-improvement”, and says a nuclear arms control-style treaty is needed to halt the technology.
“AI that can build itself would be a major development in the history of technology – one that could bring enormous good for the world in science, healthcare and beyond. But full recursive self-improvement also might increase the risks of humans losing control over AI systems,” Anthropic executives have said.
“We can’t predict what that would mean for most humans’ daily lives.” In a lengthy blog post, Anthropic co-founder Jack Clark and Anthropic Institute lead Marina Favaro called for a global pause in AI development but conceded such a halt would be tricky.
“If it were possible to effectively slow the development of this technology to give ourselves more time to deal with its immense implications, we think that would likely be a good thing,” they wrote.
“But if a slowdown simply lets the least cautious actors catch up technologically, it could leave everyone less safe.
“Without a global co-ordination mechanism, companies and governments will have to make difficult decisions about safety while under competitive and geopolitical pressures.
None of this is necessarily impossible in principle – the world has built verification regimes for other complex technologies (e.g., the Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces Treaty) – but those regimes took decades to build both the infrastructure and the trust. We don’t have that long.” Assistant Science and Technology Minister Andrew Charlton welcomed a “more considered approach to frontier AI development” even though what form that would take was unknown.
“The pace of progress must not outstrip understanding or undermine safety across society, and no company should develop AI that is unsafe,” Dr Charlton said.
“Labor believes technology should work for people and their benefit. The Australian government will continue to work across the AI Safety Institute, our regulators and international partners to keep Australians safe.” The AI Safety Institute has not yet opened. The government initially expected it to be launched earlier this year but it has suffered several setbacks.
When it begins operating, the institute’s mandate is to help the government “keep pace with rapid developments in AI technologies, dynamically addressing emerging risks and harms”.
“As AI technology evolves, the institute will work across government to support best-practice regulation, advise where updates to legislation might be needed and co-ordinate timely and consistent action to protect Australians,” Dr Charlton said last November.
Meanwhile, Anthropic believes the gap between humans and AI “is closing fast” and the technology writes more than 80 per cent of its code.
One Anthropic employee claimed to have not written “any code myself” in five months, describing it a “crazy adventure”.
Anthropic’s coding tools, such as Claude Code, have sparked steep share price falls for software companies such as Atlassian, Xero and WiseTech Global.
“Claude-written code was somewhat worse than human-written code at Anthropic in late 2025, is roughly at parity today, and we expect it to be strictly better within the year,” Mr Clark and Ms Favaro said.
They said Claude was “getting better at proposing its own experiments” and threatened to erode human collaboration, with potentially dire consequences.
“Work (and life) ran on a gift economy of small favours between humans. ‘Can you help me get this script running?’ Each one created a little debt, a little mutual awareness. (Claude is) faster, it creates zero debt, but each of these is a lost bid for human collaboration,” Anthropic said. Mr Clark and Ms Favaro said Anthropic would be transparent in its efforts to broker a global pause. “In the coming months we will organise conversations where policymakers, researchers, civil society, and other AI companies can help answer some of the questions this piece raises, especially around full recursive self-improvement and how to create better options.”
